1: A structural note: The divine word formula opens the oracle of individual accountability. Christ is the Word who takes the covenant’s moral demand personally — he does not merely transmit commands but embodies them. “In him there is no sin” (1 John 3:5) — he alone of all human beings meets the standard that Ezekiel 18 requires, and he meets it on behalf of those who cannot.
2: A pattern: The proverb “the fathers ate sour grapes and the children’s teeth are set on edge” encodes the theology of inherited guilt from which Christ’s redemption liberates. Paul’s parallel: “in Adam all die” (1 Corinthians 15:22) — the original inherited guilt. Christ enters the inherited-guilt condition (“born of a woman, born under the law,” Galatians 4:4) to liberate those who are trapped in it, replacing the proverb of death in Adam with the proverb of life in Christ.
3: A direct revelation: “As I live, declares the Lord GOD, you shall have no more occasion to use this proverb.” YHWH abolishes the proverb of inherited guilt by decree. Christ is the one through whom the decree is enacted: “there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). The proverb of inherited death is silenced by the new covenant word of individual justification through faith in Christ.
4: A direct revelation: “Every person belongs to me — the parent and the child alike: the person who sins is the one who will die.” This verse establishes the principle that makes Christ’s substitution both necessary and possible. Christ belongs to YHWH as the righteous Son — he is the one person who does not die for his own sin. His voluntary death in the place of sinners is the supreme reversal of the principle: the sinless person dies so that the sinful person need not. “He made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God” (2 Corinthians 5:21).
5: A pattern: The righteous man who does what is “lawful and right” is the legal ideal of the covenant. Christ is the only person who perfectly embodies this ideal — the truly righteous man of Ezekiel’s taxonomy, walking in all the statutes and keeping all the ordinances (Ezekiel 18:5-9). His perfect righteousness, credited to believers through faith, is the ground of justification (Romans 5:19: “by the one man’s obedience the many will be made righteous”).
6: A pattern: The righteous man’s abstinence from mountain-shrine eating, idolatry, and sexual violation represents the comprehensive covenant faithfulness that Jesus perfectly embodied. Jesus ate with sinners and tax collectors but never compromised with idolatry; he engaged women in public conversation but never violated sexual boundaries. The righteous-man portrait in Ezekiel 18:6-9 is Christological: it describes the covenant fidelity that Christ alone fulfills completely.
7: A pattern: The righteous man’s social ethics — returning the pledge, not robbing, feeding the hungry, clothing the naked — are exactly the ethics Christ both teaches and practices. The Sermon on the Mount extends these ethics; Jesus’s parable of the sheep and goats (Matthew 25:31-46) makes the feeding/clothing of the vulnerable the criterion of judgment. Christ fulfills the righteous man’s social ethics and embodies them as the standard of the kingdom.
8: A pattern: Not lending at interest, rendering honest judgment — the financial and judicial ethics of the righteous man. Christ overturns the money-changers’ tables in the temple (Matthew 21:12-13), a prophetic protest against economic exploitation in the sacred precincts. His “render to Caesar what is Caesar’s, and to God what is God’s” (Matthew 22:21) is the quintessential honest-judgment statement on the justice of temporal and divine claims.
9: A direct revelation: “He has walked in my statutes and kept my ordinances faithfully — he is just; he shall surely live.” This verdict — “he shall surely live” — is pronounced over the righteous man who fulfills the covenant. Christ alone perfectly qualifies for this verdict; his resurrection is its definitive pronouncement. The resurrection is YHWH’s own verdict of “surely live” pronounced over the one who walked in all the divine statutes — and through justification, this verdict is extended to all who are in him (Romans 4:25: “he was raised for our justification”).
10: A structural note: The wicked son of a righteous father is the anti-type of Christ’s relationship to the Father. Where the rebellious son inverts the father’s righteousness, Christ perfectly reflects the Father’s character: “whoever has seen me has seen the Father” (John 14:9). The possibility of the wicked son highlights by contrast the unique faithfulness of the Son who always does what pleases the Father (John 8:29).
11: A structural note: The wicked son eats at mountain shrines and defiles his neighbor’s wife — the two signature covenant violations (idolatry and sexual immorality) that Jesus explicitly identifies as heart-level sins (Matthew 5:28: looking with lust is adultery in the heart; Matthew 6:24: serving two masters is practical idolatry). Christ addresses the inner-life roots that produce the wicked son’s external covenant violations.
12: A structural note: The wicked son oppresses the poor and needy — the exact opposite of the righteous man’s social ethics. Jesus announces his mission as good news to the poor (Luke 4:18), releasing the oppressed. The socioeconomic reversal of the kingdom (the poor are blessed, the rich are warned — Luke 6:20-26) is the eschatological reversal of the wicked-son pattern.
13: A structural note: “His blood is upon his own head” — the principle of self-incurred blood-guilt for covenant violation. On the cross, the crowd says “his blood be on us and on our children” (Matthew 27:25) — a self-cursing formula. But Christ’s blood, rather than remaining on those who bear it, is the blood that cleanses: “the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin” (1 John 1:7). The curse-blood formula is reversed by the atonement.
14: A pattern: The righteous grandson who sees the father’s sins and does not repeat them represents moral self-determination independent of family pattern. Christ comes from a lineage of sinners (the genealogy of Matt 1 includes Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba, and the Babylonian deportation), but his righteousness is his own — not derived from ancestral purity. He is “holy, blameless, unstained, separated from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26) despite his fully human genealogy.
15: A structural note: The righteous grandson’s catalog of virtues mirrors the righteous grandfather’s, establishing that covenant faithfulness is individually chosen, not inherited. Christ’s righteousness is the supreme instance of this principle: he chose the Father’s will at every point (Gethsemane: “not my will, but yours be done,” Luke 22:42), making his righteousness a fully willed act, not a mechanical necessity.
16: A structural note: The righteous grandson does not withhold the pledge and feeds the hungry — positive enactments of covenant solidarity that mirror Christ’s provision. The multiplication of loaves (Matthew 14:19-21), the Cana wine (John 2:1-11), and the post-resurrection breakfast on the shore (John 21:9-13) are Christ’s feeding-the-hungry enacted on behalf of those who hunger for life, not merely bread.
17: A direct revelation: “He shall not die for his father’s iniquity; he shall surely live.” The verdict of life over the righteous regardless of ancestral sin is the covenantal principle that Christ enacts universally. In Christ, the believer does not die for Adam’s iniquity but lives by Christ’s righteousness: “for as by a man came death, by a man has come also the resurrection of the dead. For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive” (1 Corinthians 15:21-22).
18: A structural note: The wicked father dies in his own iniquity, with no cover from the son’s righteousness. Christ’s righteousness, however, does cover the guilty — but only on the basis of personal faith-union, not on the basis of family relationship. The difference between Ezekiel’s verdict (no coverage) and the gospel (coverage through faith in Christ) is not a contradiction but a deepening: Christ creates the possibility that the father can be covered if he also turns.
19: A structural note: The people’s objection — why does the son not bear the father’s iniquity? — reveals the expectation that family solidarity runs both ways. Christ voluntarily accepts the iniquity he was under no moral obligation to bear: “he was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities” (Isaiah 53:5). The substitution is not an application of Ezekiel’s principle but its voluntary surpassing: the sinless Son bears what the son of this chapter is released from.
20: A direct revelation: “The person who sins is the one who will die. The son will not bear the iniquity of the father.” This is the principle Christ inverts in the atonement — not by violating the moral law but by fulfilling it from the inside. As the only truly righteous Son, he had the moral capital to give himself for others: “for our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin” (2 Corinthians 5:21). The one who had no iniquity to die for became the one who died for all iniquity.
21: A direct revelation: “If the wicked man turns from all his sins... he will surely live.” This verse is the OT statement of the gospel: repentance from sin, keeping YHWH’s statutes, life as the result. Christ announces the same gospel: “repent and believe in the gospel” (Mark 1:15); “the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10). The difference in the NT is that the statutes-keeping is enabled by the Spirit and credited through Christ’s perfect righteousness, not by the sinner’s own performance.
22: A direct revelation: “All the transgressions he has committed will not be held against him.” This is Ezekiel’s anticipation of the NT doctrine of justification: transgressions are not counted against the one who turns. Paul makes the same point in the language of imputation (Romans 4:7-8, citing Psalms 32:1-2: “blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin”). The non-counting of transgressions is possible only because they are counted against Christ instead.
23: A direct revelation: “Do I have any pleasure in the death of the wicked? Is it not rather that he should turn from his ways and live?” YHWH’s desire is for repentance and life, not death and judgment. This is the theological foundation of the incarnation: the God who takes no pleasure in the death of the wicked sends his Son so that “whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life” (John 3:16). “God is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance” (2 Peter 3:9) — Ezekiel’s desire expressed as the divine motive for the cross.
24: A structural note: The righteous person who turns to wickedness dies in their iniquity. The possibility of apostasy is a serious NT concern as well (Hebrews 6:4-6; Ezekiel 10:26-31). Christ’s warning in the Gospels about those who hear the word but fall away (the parable of the sower, Mark 4:5-6, 16-17) is Ezekiel’s conversion-principle applied to the kingdom context: the good news received does not guarantee perseverance apart from continued faith.
25: A structural note: The complaint “the way of the Lord is not fair” reflects the persistent human objection to divine moral accounting. Christ engages this objection in the parable of the laborers (Matthew 20:1-16): “or do you begrudge my generosity? So the last will be first, and the first last.” YHWH’s ways appear unfair to those who expect automatic reward for accumulated moral credit — but Christ’s grace is even more radical than Ezekiel’s justice.
26: A structural note: The righteous person who turns and dies in their iniquity — the symmetric verdict. Christ’s letter to the church at Ephesus (Revelation 2:4-5) applies the same logic: “you have abandoned the love you had at first. Remember therefore from where you have fallen; repent, and do the works you did at first. If not, I will come to you and remove your lampstand from its place.” The possibility of losing what was previously held is the NT form of this warning.
27: A direct revelation: “When a wicked person turns from the wickedness they committed and does what is lawful and right, they will save their own life.” The life-saving dynamic of repentance points forward to Christ’s parable of the prodigal son: “and he arose and came to his father” (Luke 15:20). The turn toward righteousness that Ezekiel describes is, in the NT, a turn toward the Father through Christ, whose righteousness provides the ground of the restored standing.
28: A structural note: “Because he considered his ways and turned from all the transgressions he committed.” The double movement of moral reflection and turning is the structure of NT repentance. Paul’s description of the Corinthians’ repentance uses the same structure: “what earnestness this godly grief has produced in you, what eagerness to clear yourselves, what indignation, what fear, what longing, what zeal, what punishment!” (2 Corinthians 7:11). Christ’s call to repentance includes both the considering (“count the cost,” Luke 14:28) and the turning.
29: A structural note: The repeated complaint “the way of the Lord is not fair” followed by YHWH’s counter — “are not your ways the ones that are unfair?” — is the moral accountability reversal that Christ enacts in his ministry. Jesus’s interlocutors consistently find his moral accounting surprising: the workers hired last are paid the same (Matthew 20:12), the younger son is celebrated while the elder sulks (Luke 15:28-30). YHWH’s ways appear unfair only to those still calculating in terms of merit.
30: A direct revelation: “Repent and turn from all your transgressions, so that iniquity will not be your downfall.” This is the OT form of the gospel command. Christ’s first public word is “repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand” (Matthew 4:17) — the same imperative, now accompanied by the arrival of the one who makes repentance not merely obligatory but possible and fruitful. The “stumbling block” (mikshol) of iniquity that Ezekiel warns against is what Christ removes: “blessed is the one who is not offended by me” (Matthew 11:6).
31: A direct revelation: “Make yourselves a new heart and a new spirit — for why should you die, O house of Israel?” The command to make a new heart and spirit points to the new covenant promise of Ezekiel 36:26 where YHWH provides what he here commands. Christ is the mediator of the new covenant (Hebrews 9:15) through whom both the command and the provision are fulfilled: “unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God” (John 3:3) — the new heart is the new birth from above, accomplished through the Spirit sent by the risen Christ.
32: A direct revelation: “I have no pleasure in the death of anyone who dies, declares the Lord GOD. Therefore turn and live!” The final verse of Ezekiel 18 is the OT’s most concentrated expression of the gospel invitation. The two-word Hebrew command shuvu vichyu (turn and live) is the gospel in miniature. John 3:16 expands it: the God who takes no pleasure in the death of sinners gives his Son so that turning toward him brings eternal life rather than the death that sin earns. “Turn and live” becomes “believe and live” when the Son is revealed as the object of turning.
